Q1: I kept things pretty loose, honestly. I'd usually carve out about 20 hours a week, but I spread it around based on what felt right. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time making diagrams and color-coded notes from the lecture videos. I didn't follow a strict schedule—more like I'd batch my studying when I had the energy and space to focus.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was coming up. If there was a deadline looming, I'd crank up the intensity, but otherwise I'd study when it fit around my other commitments. I've got extracurriculars that take up time, so I had to be flexible. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, other weeks I'd go hard for several days straight.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or needing to grab a specific resource like a video or assignment brief. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if anything new was posted, but honestly, I didn't find myself drawn to it otherwise. The LMS was more of a tool I used when I needed something specific rather than a place I'd hang out.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. It never became automatic for me. I'd remember to check it when assignments were due or when I needed to watch a lecture, but it wasn't like brushing my teeth or anything. I think because I downloaded a lot of stuff to study offline, I didn't need to be logged in constantly.
Q5: The lecture videos were gold for me—I watched over two hours' worth because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the visual content. The discussion forums were also decent when people actually engaged with interesting questions. I found the peer interactions helpful too; bouncing ideas off others made concepts stick better.
Q6: I definitely ignored some of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. With limited resources, I couldn't always print things out, and reading long documents on screen just bored me to death. If something wasn't visual or interactive, I'd often skip it or skim it at best. Some optional quizzes I just never got around to either.
Q7: If I could see how it connected to the exam or assignments, I'd put in the effort. Activities that were visually engaging or had clear practical applications felt worth my time. But if something seemed like busywork or repetitive, I'd lose interest fast. I needed to feel like I was actually learning something useful, not just checking boxes.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. When you're juggling limited resources and other responsibilities, you prioritize what's graded. Required activities got done because they had to, but optional ones? I'd only do them if I was genuinely curious or had extra time, which wasn't often.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded videos and PDFs to study on my phone during commutes or when my internet was spotty. I also used YouTube tutorials and free resources online to supplement what we were learning. A lot of my actual studying happened with handwritten notes and diagrams I'd made myself, completely offline.
Q10: There were tons of times I'd be working through problems on paper or reviewing my handwritten notes for hours, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also discuss concepts with classmates in person or over messaging apps. The LMS only captured a fraction of what I was actually doing to learn the material.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture video once, then watch it multiple times offline while taking notes. Same with assignment briefs—I'd save them and refer back to them without logging in again. My actual engagement with those materials was way higher than my login count would suggest.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough. I was feeling pretty bored with the content, and my stress levels were through the roof from everything piling up. There were weeks where I just couldn't bring myself to care about the course material. The CBT sessions helped me manage the stress somewhat, but motivation was still a struggle.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from extracurriculars or stressed about money stuff, the LMS was the last thing on my mind. I'd do the bare minimum to get by during those periods. Confusion about certain topics also made me avoid those sections rather than diving in and figuring them out.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd have a video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. Those sessions felt like wasted time in retrospect, but I was too mentally drained to engage properly.
Q15: I put off the longer reading assignments constantly, even though I knew they'd help with exams. They just felt overwhelming and boring, so I'd convince myself I'd get to them later. Sometimes 'later' never came. The visual stuff I'd do right away, but dense text? That got pushed to the bottom of the list.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated my entire LMS usage pattern. I'd have bursts of activity right before something was due, then go quiet for a while. It wasn't ideal, but it worked for me. The pressure of a deadline was often the only thing that could cut through my boredom and get me moving.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it motivated me to engage more with similar activities. But if feedback was delayed or vague, I'd lose interest in that aspect of the course. Quick, specific feedback made me feel like my effort was worth something. Without it, I'd just move on to whatever was next.
Q18: The course structure was decent but didn't always match how I learn best. Weekly modules helped me pace myself somewhat, but the heavy reliance on reading materials didn't suit my visual learning style. I participated more when there were videos or interactive elements involved.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier—I could actually stay focused on those. But the sheer amount of text-based resources made it harder. Also, having too many optional activities without clear guidance on what was most valuable left me feeling a bit lost about where to direct my energy.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for what they need without finding it, or they're trying to understand the system itself. Others might be like me on bad days—logged in but not really present. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity; sometimes it's just clicking around aimlessly.
Q21: That's basically me. My attendance was pretty low and I wasn't constantly on the LMS, but I still pulled a Merit grade. I studied efficiently offline, used external resources, and focused my energy on what actually mattered for assessments. The LMS numbers don't tell the whole story of how someone learns.
Q22: Logs can show patterns like when you're most active or which resources you click on. But they miss the quality of engagement—whether you're actually thinking deeply or just skimming. They also miss all the offline work, peer discussions outside the platform, and the mental effort that doesn't leave a digital trace.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actually thinking about the material, making connections, and caring about understanding it—not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious and want to dig deeper, not just checking things off a list. True engagement is mental investment, which doesn't always look like constant activity.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with stress this semester or how the CBT sessions affected my ability to focus. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with handwritten notes or discussing concepts with friends. And it definitely wouldn't show that despite feeling bored a lot of the time, I still found ways to learn effectively on my own terms.
